coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of yellowish-green emitting carbon quantum dots and their applications for phoxim sensing and cellular imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Huo, Xingyan
Liu, Lizhen
Bai, Yunfeng
Qin, Jun
Yuan, Lin
Feng, Feng
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we reported the first use of a novel fluorescent probe for phoxim detection based on yellowish-green emissive carbon quantum dots (CQDs). By facile one-pot hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and p-phenylenediamine, the CQDs with yellowish-green luminescence were synthesized. The sensing mechanism is based on the quenching effect of phoxim on the fluorescence emission of CQDs by inner filter effect (IFE). The CQDs showed a good sensitivity and selectivity to phoxim detection and dual good linear relationships were provided in the concentration ranges of 1–10 μM and 10–98 μM, associated with the limit of detection of 0.09 μM. The proposed assay has been allowed to detect phoxim in real samples with satisfying recoveries ranging from 93 to 105.9%. Furthermore, due to the low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility, the as-prepared CQDs was successfully used for cell imaging, demonstrating the considerable potential applications of such CQDs in biological field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Phoxim
Fluorescent probe
Cellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of conditions for phoxim determination

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and (B) size distribution histogram of CQDs.
XPS spectra of (A) CQDs and the deconvoluted details (B) C1s, (C) N1s, and (D) O1s.

(A) UV–vis absorption (black curve), the fluorescence excitation (red curve) and emission (blue curve) spectra of CQDs aqueous solution. Inset shows the photographs of the CQDs solution under (left) daylight and under (right) UV light (365 nm). (B) The PL spectra of CQDs under various excitation wavelengths, ranging from 350 to 450 nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs at different phoxim concentrations (0–98 μM). (B) and (C) are the linear relationship between F0/F and the concentration of phoxim. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three replicate experiments.

(A) The F0/F of the CQDs before (F0) and after (F) the addition of various interfering substances. (B) The F0/F of a CQDs-phoxim sensing system measured before and after mixing with various competing substances (concentration of phoxim is 0.1 mg mL−1, concentrations of all other competing substances are 10 mg mL−1). Error bars are estimated from three independent measurements.

Laser confocal scanning microscopy images of HeLa cells treated with only medium (control cells) and the CQDs (300 μg mL−1) for 2 h in yellow channel, red channel and the corresponding bright-field images and overlay images. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Determination results of phoxim in real samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of yellowish-green emitting carbon quantum dots and their applications for phoxim sensing and cellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen, 041004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen 041004, PRChina

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Shanxi Datong University, Datong, 037009, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we reported the first use of a novel fluorescent probe for phoxim detection based on yellowish-green emissive carbon quantum dots (CQDs). By facile one-pot hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and p-phenylenediamine, the CQDs with yellowish-green luminescence were synthesized. The sensing mechanism is based on the quenching effect of phoxim on the fluorescence emission of CQDs by inner filter effect (IFE). The CQDs showed a good sensitivity and selectivity to phoxim detection and dual good linear relationships were provided in the concentration ranges of 1–10 μM and 10–98 μM, associated with the limit of detection of 0.09 μM. The proposed assay has been allowed to detect phoxim in real samples with satisfying recoveries ranging from 93 to 105.9%. Furthermore, due to the low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility, the as-prepared CQDs was successfully used for cell imaging, demonstrating the considerable potential applications of such CQDs in biological field.

ce:section.ce:para: Phoxim (phenylgloxylonitrile oxime o, o-diethyl phosphorothionate, Fig. S1), an organophosphate pesticide, is widely used in agricultural fields to defend farm against pests and insects. However, the excessive use of phoxim causes a large number of phoxim residues in food, water and soil, which pose serious threat to the ecosystem balance and human health. Also, phoxim can irreversibly inhibit the catalytic activity of acetylcholinesterase, bringing about the aggregation of the neurotransmitter acetylcholine and the impact or even harm brain function, cognitive competence and living ability of human beings, subsequently [1,2]. Owing to its high poisonousness, very rigid legal regulations have been adopted to curb its residual content. For instance, in China, the maximum concentrations of phoxim allowed in cereals, vegetables and fruits should be lower than 0.05 mg kg−1 [3], whereas, in Europe Union, the maximum residual levels in chicken, sheep and porcine muscles are 25, 50, and 20 μg kg−1, respectively [4]. However, unfortunately, these levels are constantly exceeded in developing countries where the utilization of pesticides is often misused and unmonitored. For these reasons, the development of a reliable, inexpensive and sensitive analytical technique for phoxim detection is highly essential and necessary.

So far, numerous analytical methods, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [5,6], near-infrared spectroscopy [7], gas chromatography (GC) [8], UV–vis spectrometry [9], amperometric biosensor [10], host-guest chemistry [11] and electrochemical analysis (EC) [12] have been established to detect phoxim. Most of them require sophisticated sample pretreatment processes, expensive instrumentations, or skilled operators, which largely hinder their desirable and promising applications. In comparison with these conventional methods, the fluorescence sensor strategy has attracted much more attention owing to its simple fabrication, fast response, low cost, and easy operation.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a rising generation of the carbon material family, have attracted considerable concerns because of their outstanding physiochemical properties such as low cytotoxicity, good water solubility, super anti-photobleaching, chemical inertness, high photoluminescence and excellent biocompatibility [13–16]. Such fascinating properties not only make this new class of nanoparticles better than semiconductor quantum dots and conventional organic dyes [17,18], but also provide great potential in the fields of inkjet printing dye [19], chemical sensing [20,21], drug detection [22], optoelectronic devices [23,24]and bioimaging [25]. Since their incidental discovery in 2004 [26], various approaches have been applied for the preparation of CQDs, including laser ablation [27], pyrolysis [28], electrochemical [29], hydrothermal [30,31] and microwave methods [32] using different carbon sources. However, most of them possess blue or green light emission under UV light excitation, which severely hamper their applications scope, particularly in the bioimaging fields, owing to the common blue autofluorescence in the biological matrix and severe photodamage caused by UV excitation light [33,34]. To overcome these shortcomings, several CQDs with long-wavelength emission have been reported [35,36]. And, as we expected, these yellow or red emissive CQDs indeed exhibit high sensitivity and selectivity for target, such as Fe3+, In3+ and Pd2+ [37,38], tetracycline [39], l-cysteine [40], glutathione [41], malachite green [42] and etc. Nevertheless, as far as we known, no work has been reported that the long-wavelength-emitting CQDs were used for phoxim detection so far.

In this article, for the first time, we demonstrated the use of a yellowish-green emissive CQDs as a fluorescence probe for the determination of phoxim. The CQDs were fabricated by a facile hydrothermal carbonization of citric acid and p-phenylenediamine. The as-prepared CQDs possess good water-solubility, lower cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility and strong fluorescence. Phoxim shows robust quenching effect on yellowish-green emission of CQDs. On the basis of this quenching effect, a new fluorescent sensor for the determination of phoxim was established. The probe further appeared high accuracy and was successfully applied to detect phoxim in the real samples without any interference. Additionally, these yellowish-green emitting CQDs have been applied for cell imaging to guarantee their further application in bio-systems.

Phoxim, glyphosate, carbendazim, ascorbic acid, chlorpyrifos and all amino acids were supplied by Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Citric acid, disodium phosphate (Na2HPO4), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), hydrochloric acid (HCl), quinine sulfate, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), p-phenylenediamine were received from Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). NaCl, CaCl2, KCl, MnCl2, CoCl2, AlCl3, CrCl3, ZnCl2, BaCl2, MgCl2 and CdCl2 were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). HeLa cells were purchased from iCell Bioscience Inc. (Shanghai, China). Methyl tetrazolium (MTT) and minimum eagle medium (MEM) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was purchased from Procell Life Science &Technology Co., Ltd (Wuhan, China). All reagents used were of at least analytical grade. Ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ) was used throughout this work.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was obtained on a Tecnai F30 microscope (FEI, USA). Fluorescent excitation and emission spectra were measured on a F-2500 spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Lambda 35 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) measurement was performed on an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fischer, USA). Fluorescence lifetime measurements were recorded by using a QM8000 spectrofluorometer (Horiba Scientific, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was carried out on a Nicolet iS5 spectrometer (Thermo Fischer, USA), and fluorescence images were taken by laser confocal scanning microscopy (Olympus, Japan).

The CQDs were synthesized through one-pot hydrothermal route according to a previously reported method with some modifications [43,44]. In detail, 0.5 g of p-phenylenediamine and 0.5 g of citric acid were mixed with 20 mL of deionized water under ultrasonic dissolution for 3 min. Subsequently, the uniform solution was transferred into a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) autoclaves and reacted at 200 °C for 8 h. After natural cooling, the brownish-yellow crude product was dialyzed against ultrapure water for 24 h through a dialysis bag (MWCO: 500 Da) to get rid of the unreacted chemical materials. Finally, the CQDs powder were collected by freeze drying and stored at 4 °C for further research and characterization.

The measurements of phoxim were performed in 0.01 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH = 4). Briefly, 300 μL 15 mg mL−1 CQDs solution and different volumes of phoxim stock solution were transferred into a colorimetric tube, and then diluted to 3 mL using 0.01 M PBS solution (pH = 4). After reacting for 5 min, the corresponding emission spectra were measured at 360 nm excitation wavelength. Fluorescence intensity ratio F0/F was analyzed（F0 and F were the fluorescence intensity of CQDs before and after the addition of phoxim, respectively). Each sample was measured in parallel three times. The selectivity of phoxim sensing was evaluated by adding other interfering substances to substitute phoxim in a similar manner.

The real samples (celery, cucumber, apple, tea and broccoli) were obtained from local supermarket in Shanxi Datong. 40 g of each sample was cleaned and homogenized using a grinder. Then the mixture was centrifuged (8000 rpm, 5 min) to remove additional deposits. Subsequently, the resulting supernatant was taken out and passed through a 0.44 μm microporous membrane. Finally, the filtrate was collected and reserved under the condition of 4.0 °C for further analysis.

MTT assay was carried out to assess the influence of CQDs on HeLa cells viability. Briefly, Hela cells were incubated in a 96-well plate at 1✕105 mL−1 density, 100 μL medium (MEM containing 10% FBS) was added per well. After overnight growth at 37 °C (5% CO2), the supernatant was removed and the cells were exposed to the culture medium containing different concentrations of CQDs (100–500 μg mL−1). The cells treated with only medium were used as control. After another 24 h, the supernatant was discarded, and fresh culture medium containing 15 μL of MTT (5 mg mL−1) was added into all wells and further incubated for 4 h. Finally, the culture medium was discarded and 150 μL of DMSO was put in each well. After 10 min, the absorbance (I) of each well was recorded by a microplate reader (Infinite M200PRO, Switzerland). The cell viability was defined as the ratio of the absorbance in the presence of CQDs to that in the absence of CQDs (control). Cell viability (%)= (I sample/I control) ✕100%.

The Hela cells were seeded into glass bottom culture dishes for 24 h at 37 °C with 5% CO2. Then, the cells were treated with CQDs (300 μg mL−1) for 2 h. Subsequently, the cells were washed 3 times by PBS to remove medium and the free CQDs. Finally, the cells were imaged by laser confocal scanning microscopy (LCSM).

TEM analysis was employed to characterize the morphology and size of fluorescent CQDs. As shown in Fig. 1 A, CQDs display a quasi-spherical structure and are monodispersed. Meanwhile, the histogram (Fig. 1B) shows that the sizes of CQDs are mainly distributed in the range of 0.7–6.7 nm and the mean lateral size is about 3.6 nm by measuring outnumber 80 random nanoparticles.

To better understand the surface-functional groups of CQDs, the FTIR spectrum was carried out (Fig. S2). The broad absorption peaks at 3341 cm−1and 3221 cm−1 arouse from the O–H and N–H stretching vibrations, whereas the peak at 2930 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of C–H group. The peak at 1699 cm−1 indicates the presence of a typical CO group. The band at 1665 cm−1 is from the stretching vibration of CC group and that at 1517 cm−1 is related with the stretching vibration of CN group. Specifically, the peaks at 1395 cm−1 and 1265 cm−1 are indicative of C–N stretching vibration and peak at 1129 cm−1 is recognized as the stretching vibration of C–O group. The peak around 830 cm−1 belongs to the bending vibration of N–H group. The above FTIR results reveal that the yellowish-green fluorescence CQDs are with –NH2, –COOH and –OH surface functional groups. Moreover, XPS spectrum was utilized to investigate the surface state and elemental compositions of CQDs. The wide-scan XPS spectrum (Fig. 2 A) of CQDs confirms the existence of carbon and oxygen along with the dopant nitrogen at peak position 284.8eV for C1s, 531.64 eV for O1s and 399.56eV for N1s.The C1s peak (Fig. 2B) is deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.54 eV (CC/C–C), 285.5 eV (C–N) and 288.07 eV (CO). The N1s band (Fig. 2C) shows two peaks centered at 399.19 and 399.97 eV corresponding to the CN–C and C–N–C groups. The O1s band (Fig. 2D) exhibits three peaks at 531.54,532.47 and 533.28 eV, which are attributed to CO, C–OH/C–O–C and O–H groups, respectively. Specifically, the XPS analysis shows no difference with the FTIR results, showing that nitrogen was successfully incorporated into CQDs. Simultaneously, these characterizations proved that the abundant hydrophilic moieties exist on the surface of CQDs, which can largely raise water-solubility of CQDs.

The PL spectra and UV–vis absorption were analyzed to explore the optical properties of the CQDs. As shown in Fig. 3 A, an evident UV–vis absorption peak of the CQDs at 243 nm was recorded (black curve), which can be appointed to the π−π∗ transition of CC bond. The color of obtained CQDs solution is brown under visible light but shows a strong yellowish-green when illuminating under a 365 nm UV lamp (inset of Fig. 3A). Upon excitation at 360 nm (the optimal λex), the highest PL emission at 496 nm was observed, (Fig. 3A, red and blue curves). In addition，a series of emission spectra of the CQDs (Fig. 3B) under different excitation wavelengths were recorded. The fluorescence emission wavelength of CQDs is found to be excitation wavelength-dependent. Namely, when the CQDs are excited at different wavelengths (350–450 nm), the emission peak shows the red-shift of 40 nm (495–535 nm). This is generally attributed to the different surface states and non-uniform sizes impacting the band gap of CQDs [45,46]. The fluorescence quantum yield (Φs) of CQDs was estimated to be 8.55% (Fig. S3, Supporting Information), using quinine sulfate as a standard.

Since the stability of CQDs is of great significance for phoxim sensing and cell imaging, it was investigated under different NaCl concentration and ultraviolet irradiation time. Fig. S4A displays the effect of ionic strength on the PL intensity of CQDs. The PL intensity and spectral feature of CQDs do not change much under different concentrations of NaCl which is beneficial since it is necessary for CQDs to be used in the presence of various salt concentrations in practical applications. The photobleaching experiment was processed under the excitation wavelength at 360 nm. As shown in Fig. S4B, after 180 min of continuous UV excitation, the fluorescence just slightly decreases, which indicates that CQDs have satisfying photobleaching resistance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of conditions for phoxim determination
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the optimal sensing condition of fluorescent probe and promote the detection sensitivity of phoxim, the effects of experimental parameters, including the dosages of CQDs, pH and incubation time on the fluorescence intensity ratio (F0/F) were studied in this work.

Firstly, we investigated the influence of CQDs dosage with the volumes ranging from 50 to 500 μL on F0/F. It is clear that the F0/F gradually increases as the CQDs dosage increased from 50 to 300 μL, and then decreases in the high dosage range (350–500 μL). Therefore, 300 μL was chosen as the best dosage of CQDs for the following study (Fig. S5A).

Then, the influence of pH on the synthesized CQDs was examined in different buffer solution (pH 2-12) as displayed in Fig. S5B. When the pH value was about 4.0, the maximum value of F0/F was observed, thus pH = 4.0 was regarded as the best value.

Furthermore, the suitable incubation time for phoxim detection was investigated in the time range of 0–60 min. As shown in Fig. S5C, after adding phoxim, the F0/F increases sharply within 5 min and then keep almost constant. Therefore, the optimal reaction time should be at least 5 min.

Under the optimized condition, the sensing performance of CQDs to phoxim was carefully studied. As the phoxim concentration increases from 0 μmol L−1 to 98 μmol L−1, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs gradually declines (Fig. 4 A), revealing that the sensing system is sensitive to phoxim. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F0/F) is plotted against the concentration of phoxim to test the linearity of this fluorescent sensor for phoxim detection. It can be seen that F0/F of CQDs shows two good linear relationships to the phoxim concentration in ranges of 1–10 μM and 10–98 μM ((Fig. 4B and C) with the linear regression equations: F0/F = 0.0587[C]+1 (R2 = 0.9969) and F0/F = 0.0263[C]+1.275 (R2 = 0.9981), respectively, where [C] is the concentration of phoxim. The limits of detection (LOD) are estimated to be 0.09 μM according to 3σ/m (σ is the standard deviation of blank solution (n = 10) and m is the slope of the calibration curve). We made a comparison between the as-constructed FL sensor with some other reported techniques for the phoxim detection (Table S1). Clearly, our method exhibits comparable or even lower LOD than other methods except the HPLC detection. Although HPLC methods have a lower LOD, they involve expensive instruments and time-consuming procedures. On the contrary, our developed strategy based on CQDs is very fast, easy and cost-effective, which should be an excellent platform for the detection of phoxim.

The sensing mechanism for phoxim was discussed by UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra and fluorescence decay curves. Fig. S6A showed UV-via absorption spectra of CQDs, phoxim, CQDs-phoxim. The absorption spectrum of CQDs-phoxim system (curve c) was same as sum absorbance (curve d) of curve a and b, indicating that there was not chemical reaction between them. Static quenching requires the formation of nonfluorescent ground-state complex between the quencher and fluorophore, which can change the absorption spectrum of CQDs-phoxim system. So, static quenching process was ruled out. In addition, the decay lifetimes of CQDs in the absence and presence of phoxim were investigated and shown in Fig. S6B. The average fluorescence lifetimes of CQDs and phoxim- CQDs were calculated to be 8.41 ns and 8.87 ns, respectively, meaning that the fluorescence lifetime of CQDs was almost unchanged after incubation with phoxim. This result excludes the dynamic quenching mechanism and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism, because the lifetime of CQDs will be decreased when the two mechanisms exist. Fig. S6C showed that the absorption spectrum of phoxim overlaps the fluorescence spectra of CQDs. Both the FRET and IFE processes, excellent spectral overlap between the absorption spectrum of a quencher and the excitation or emission spectrum of a fluorophore must be produced, but the fluorescence lifetime excludes the FRET mechanism, so IFE was speculated as the quenching mechanism of CQDs.

To further confirm the IFE quenching effect, several UV–vis absorption spectra of interferences, including Na+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Ba2+, Al3+, Cd2+, glutamic acid, dl-methionine, ascorbic acid, glycine, l-cysteine, carbendazim, glyphosate, and chlorpyrifos are displayed in Fig. S7. It is obvious that only the UV–vis absorption spectrum of phoxim have the significant overlap with the excitation and emission spectra of CQDs, and other possible interfering species show inconspicuous overlap, illustrating it is phoxim that shields excitation and emission light from CQDs. The results demonstrate that the built method has a high selectivity toward phoxim.

To investigate selectivity, the fluorescence responses of a CQDs solution towards the introduction of various possible interfering species, including different metal ions, amino acids and small molecules were investigated. As depicted in Fig. 5 A, only phoxim showed dramatic quenching effect on the fluorescence of CQDs while the other possible interfering species showed inconspicuous influence on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs.

In the anti-interference ability assessment, the influences of various possible competing species, i.e., different metal ions, amino acids and small molecules, on the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs-phoxim sensing system were recorded. As shown in Fig. 5B, no obvious changes were caused by the introduction of competing substances, revealing that the proposed sensing system exhibited excellent anti-interference ability for phoxim detection.

To test the practical ability of this developed sensing system, the CQDs-based fluorescent probe was further applied for phoxim determination in real samples, i.e. celery, cucumber, apple, tea and broccoli samples. Table 1 summarizes the analytical results of phoxim in real samples. The recoveries for the detection of phoxim in real samples are in the range of 93–105.9% with RSD (n = 3) of 1.91–4.21%. The results reveal that our proposed method can be successfully applied to detect phoxim in real samples with excellent repeatability and high applicability.

Fig. 6 showed the viability study of different concentrations of CQDs by MTT assay against Hela cells. After 24 h, the cell survival rate is still >80%, even the concentration of CQDs was up to 500 μg mL−1. Such phenomena confirmed that the CQDs has excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity therefore are fit to do cell imaging. Fig. 7 presented the LCSM images of Hela cells after being incubated with CQDs (300 μg mL−1) for 2 h. The fabricated CQDs displayed bright yellow and red fluorescence emission in the intracellular region when excited at 405 and 559 nm, respectively. However, under the identical exposure conditions, the control cells (without CQDs) had no observable fluorescence. These findings indicated that the CQDs are successfully distributed into the cells in short time. Therefore, the CQDs are expected to be of great interest for bioimaging applications.

In summary, a rapid and reliable fluorescent sensor based on yellowish-green emissive CQDs for phoxim detection was successfully developed. The hydrothermal synthesis of the CQDs is easy, economical, fast and green. The average size of the obtained CQDs is 3.6 nm, exhibiting a bright yellowish-green emission under a 365 nm UV lamp irradiation with quantum yield of 8.55%. Phoxim, as a fluorescence quencher, induced quenching of the fluorescence of CQDs, and such quenching behaviors are due to the large absorption of phoxim on the excitation and emission luminescence of CQDs. The fluorescent probe developed here can effectively detect phoxim with two good linear ranges (1–10 μM and 10–98 μM) and highly sensitivity (0.09 μM). Meanwhile, satisfactory results were achieved when detecting phoxim in real samples, proving the feasibility of this method. More significantly, the obtained CQDs exhibited low toxicity and good cell penetration ability, and was applied for imaging of Hela cells. For the easy fabrication of stable and strong fluorescence of CQDs, our study may offer a potential avenue for the detection of phoxim and bio-imaging.

Xingyan Huo: Methodology, Validation, Writing, Visualization. Lizhen Liu: Supervision, Validation, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yunfeng Bai: Investigation. Jun Qin: Investigation. Lin Yuan: Investigation. Feng Feng: Investigation, Validation, Investigation, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial supports from Applied Basic Research Project Fund of Shanxi Province (201901D211434) and Higher Education Technology Innovation Project of Shanxi Province (2019L0750), all of which are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2021.338685.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Environmental behaviors of phoxim with two formulations in bamboo forest under soil surface mulching
J. Environ. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, Z. Ni, R. Mo, D. Shen, D. Zhong, F. Tang, Environmental behaviors of phoxim with two formulations in bamboo forest under soil surface mulching, J. Environ. Sci. 35 (2015) 91-100.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Characterization and expression analysis of peroxiredoxin family genes from the silkworm Bombyx mori in response to phoxim and chlorpyrifos
Pestic. Biochem. Physiol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Shi, Z. Zhang, K. Jia, K. Zhang, D. An, G. Wang, B. Zhang, H. Yin, Characterization and expression analysis of peroxiredoxin family genes from the silkworm Bombyx mori in response to phoxim and chlorpyrifos, Pestic. Biochem. Phys. 114 (2014) 24-31.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of pesticides in vegetable samples using an acetylcholinesterase biosensor based on nanoparticles ZrO2/chitosan composite film
Int. J. Environ. Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Yang, M. Guo, M. Yang, Z. Wang, G. Shen, R. Yu, Determination of pesticides in vegetable samples using an acetylcholinesterase biosensor based on nanoparticles ZrO2/chitosan composite film, Int. J. Environ. Anal. Chem. 85 (2005) 163-175.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Commission Regulation (EU) No 37/2010 of 22 December 2009 on pharmacologically active substances and their classification regarding maximum residue limits in foodstuffs of animal origin
Off. J. Eur. Union

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: European Commission (2010) Commission Regulation (EU) No 37/2010 of 22 December 2009 on pharmacologically active substances and their classification regarding maximum residue limits in foodstuffs of animal origin, Off. J. Eur. Union L15 (2010) 1-72.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Application of liquid-phase microextraction for the determination of phoxim in water samples by high performance liquid chromatography with diode array detector
Microchem. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Liang, L. Guo, Y. Liu, S. Liu, T. Zhang, Application of liquid-phase microextraction for the determination of phoxim in water samples by high performance liquid chromatography with diode array detector, Microchem. J. 80 (2005) 19-23.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of phoxim residues in eggs by using high-performance liquid chromatography diode array detection after treatment of stocked housing facilities for the poultry red mite (Dermanyssus gallinae)
Anal. Chim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Hamscher, B. P. H. Nau, Determination of phoxim residues in eggs by using high-performance liquid chromatography diode array detection after treatment of stocked housing facilities for the poultry red mite (Dermanyssus gallinae), Anal. Chim. Acta. 586 (2007) 330−335.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Direct detection of phoxim in water by two-dimensional correlation near-infrared spectroscopy combined with partial least squares discriminant analysis
